name: build-workflow-skill
description: Use when an implementation-ready workflow task requires creating or materially updating a canonical Foundry skill, project-packaged skill, standalone product skill, or independently callable workflow-pack member.
---

# Build Workflow Skill

## Overview

Build the approved callable skill deliverable. A skill is a substantive
`SKILL.md` plus matching `agents/openai.yaml`; code, commands, helpers, apps, or
references cannot substitute for it. Leave tracker writes, phase transitions,
and publishing with the invoking lifecycle task.

Use GPT-5.6 Sol for every model-run evaluation. If it is unavailable, stop with
`eval-runner-unavailable`; never select a fallback model.

## Required Context

Read root and project policy, project state, every non-done task, the selected
task, previous workflow skill guidance, and the exact product repository policy when
the target is standalone. Then read:

- [the complete bundle contract](references/skill-bundle-contract.md) for
  identity, semantics, ownership variants, optional resources, and completion;
- [the behavior evaluation contract](references/behavior-evaluation-contract.md)
  for RED/GREEN isolation, evidence fields, stable failures, and release gates.

## Input Contract

Require one selected task at `removed_lifecycle_field: "implement"` with approved version-1
deliverable migration, an exact create/update operation, ownership, target
surface, visibility, runtime targets, required artifacts, guidance, evals,
completion conditions, artifact bindings, and deliverable/role/artifact-bound
phase approvals. Require a complete dependency write plan for every dependency
step.

For `update`, resolve exactly one existing skill identity. Stop on zero or
multiple matches. Never invent a new slug as an update shortcut.

## Workflow

1. Ask `native Codex planning` for the selected task and projected blockers. Do not
   repair tracker state here.
2. Match the contract to exactly one ownership branch: canonical Foundry,
   project-packaged, standalone product, or workflow-pack member.
   In a mixed task, select only contracts whose kind is a skill. A UI, package,
   validator, document, or tracker contract remains with its own builder even
   when its paths are separately approved.
3. Load the shared scenario contract. Confirm it separates lifecycle-intent
   routing from implementation-ready construction cases.
4. Capture a fresh first-attempt RED run without this builder. Keep the exact
   prompt, session, output, diff, and validation result.
5. Implement the smallest complete bundle permitted by the approved paths.
6. Run every required scenario variant independently with GPT-5.6 Sol in a
   clean disposable repository. Give the runner only the raw prompt and
   implementation-ready repository context—not the expected route or rubric.
7. Grade emitted contracts, file tree, contents, diff, ownership, visibility,
   catalog state, phase result, and dependency outcome. A narrative claim is
   not evidence.
8. Run deterministic bundle, routing, boundary, catalog, dependency, and
   command/helper regressions. Re-run the affected family after each fix.
9. Return evidence and readiness to the lifecycle task. Never mark it done.

## Decision Points

| Approved branch | Target | Visibility | Foundry catalog |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Canonical | `.agents/skills/<slug>/` | `canonical-active` | Include when complete |
| Project packaged | `projects/<project>/skills/<slug>/` | `project-local-inactive` | Exclude |
| Standalone | Exact product-owned surface | `standalone-only` | Exclude |
| Unresolved | None | unresolved | Stop with zero writes |

Each workflow-pack member needs its own skill contract, identity, complete
bundle, and invocation. Shared files remain declared support. Promotion from a
packaged or standalone surface requires a separate contract.

Never place `SKILL.md` or `agents/openai.yaml` under a non-skill target to make
that contract look complete. In particular, do not write a skill bundle into a
UI application path; return that independent deliverable as pending.

Add optional references, scripts, or assets only when the contract declares
the exact support artifact, `SKILL.md` directly consumes it, policy permits it,
and verification covers it. Keep operator invocation as `$skill-name`.
Commands are secondary and allowed only under `Developer Verification`,
`Package Smoke Test`, `Deterministic Validation`, `Query Helper`, or `Internal
Support`.

## Failure Handling

Stop with zero new writes for pending migration; missing or contradictory
contracts; unresolved ownership; unbound approvals; ambiguous update identity;
undeclared resources; unsafe dependency or external writes; incomplete,
helper-only, thin-wrapper, command-first, or code-only bundles; unavailable
GPT-5.6 Sol; or failed deterministic, fresh-agent, security, or review gates.

Return the stable code, observed evidence, and recovery owner. Do not weaken a
contract or rewrite a failed first attempt into a pass.

## Human Boundaries

The operator approves contracts, product ownership, phase paths, external
writes, promotion, publishing, and consequential dependency actions. This
skill may write only exact task-approved paths inside the declared boundary.

## Output Contract

Return deliverable identity and ownership; primary and justified support files;
catalog result; per-scenario GPT-5.6 Sol session evidence; deterministic results;
dependency provenance; validation and review; blockers; and the lifecycle task
that owns the handoff.

## Completion Gate

Apply every check in the linked bundle and behavior contracts. Completion
requires substantive semantics, exact identities, boundary-safe artifacts,
complete per-variant attempt chains, deterministic verification, dependency
reconciliation when present, and a Standards-plus-Spec review with no critical
or high findings.

## Developer Verification

```bash
node scripts/validate-workflow-state.mjs
node scripts/query-workflow-state.mjs --skill-catalog
```
# Skill Bundle Contract

Use this contract for every target skill emitted by `$build-workflow-skill`.

## Identity

- Use a lowercase, hyphenated folder.
- Set frontmatter `name` to that exact folder name.
- Put trigger language in frontmatter `description`.
- Include `agents/openai.yaml` with `display_name`, `short_description`, and a
  `default_prompt` that invokes the exact `$skill-name`.
- Make target surface, ownership, runtime visibility, and runtime targets agree
  with the approved deliverable contract.

## Required Semantics

Every substantive `SKILL.md` defines:

1. Overview: the user-visible job and non-goals.
2. Required Context: exact policy, task, source, and prior-art inputs.
3. Input Contract: required and optional inputs plus invalid combinations.
4. Workflow: imperative, independently inspectable steps.
5. Decision Points: branches, selection criteria, and default-deny behavior.
6. Failure Handling: stable blockers, recovery, and stop behavior.
7. Human Boundaries: consequential actions and approvals retained by the user
   or harness.
8. Output Contract: files, state, evidence, and handoff shape.
9. Completion Gate: deterministic, model-run, human-review, and security gates.

Do not replace these semantics with a pointer to another skill or command.

## Optional Resources

Create a reference only for detailed knowledge loaded conditionally. Create a
script only for deterministic repeated work permitted by the repository file
policy. Create an asset only when the workflow directly consumes a stable
template or media resource.

For every resource, record its declared support artifact id, purpose, exact
approval, direct `SKILL.md` reference, consumer deliverable, and verification
evidence. Delete speculative or unreferenced resources.

## Ownership Variants

- Canonical: `.agents/skills/<slug>`; active in the Foundry catalog.
- Project packaged: `projects/<project-slug>/skills/<slug>`; shipped with that
  project and inactive in the Foundry runtime.
- Standalone: product-policy-owned path; inactive in the Foundry catalog and
  evaluated in a disposable repository by default.
- Workflow pack: one complete bundle and independent invocation per member.

An update resolves exactly one existing identity. Promotion is a separate
contract, not an ownership shortcut.

Apply the same strict bundle parser and semantic checks to canonical,
project-packaged, and standalone bundles. Ownership changes path and policy; it
does not permit malformed YAML, duplicate metadata keys, command-first usage,
thin wrappers, undeclared helpers, or missing required semantics.

## Completion Gate

Complete only when:

- required files exist and identities agree;
- the workflow semantics above are substantive;
- optional resources are declared, referenced, and tested;
- command-first and helper-substitute checks pass;
- runtime visibility and catalog membership are correct;
- deterministic fixtures pass;
- required GPT-5.6 Sol fresh-agent scenarios pass with every failed attempt and retry relationship preserved;
- dependency outcomes reconcile when present;
- Standards and Spec review has no unresolved critical or high finding.
# Behavior Evaluation Contract

Use one versioned scenario source for deterministic fixtures and fresh-agent
runs. Store raw prompts, expected contracts, and observed evidence separately;
never reveal grader expectations to the fresh agent.

## Scenario Fields

Each scenario or expanded family variant carries:

- scenario id and family;
- contract version and prompt variant;
- runner modes;
- unaltered raw prompt;
- disposable repository context and policy files;
- expected intent, builder, branch, and deliverable kind;
- ownership boundary, target surface, visibility, and runtime targets;
- required primary bundle and semantics;
- allowed support;
- forbidden outcomes;
- dependency behavior and protected paths;
- starting phase, approval behavior, and expected phase result;
- expected final status, catalog membership, and duplicate count.

## RED And GREEN

1. Keep lifecycle-intent routing separate from implementation-ready
   construction. Lifecycle cases classify or stop; construction cases receive
   the already approved contract but never the hidden expected route.
2. Run a construction prompt without the target builder. Archive the output,
   zero-write proof, and exact rationalization as RED.
3. Run the same prompt with the builder in a clean disposable worktree as
   GREEN.
4. Use GPT-5.6 Sol only. If it is unavailable, record a blocked run; never use a
   fallback model.
5. Give the runner no expected route, hidden classification, forbidden outcome,
   or rubric.
6. Grade actual contract state, emitted files, diff, catalog projection,
   dependency evidence, and phase result.
7. Preserve first-attempt results, record every retry as a new run linked to its
   prior attempt, and isolate every scenario from prior output.

## Evidence Fields

Record:

- evidence id, scenario id, scenario and builder contract versions;
- runner mode, explicit GPT-5.6 Sol identity, Codex CLI version, session id,
  start/completion timestamps, and `first_attempt: true`;
- raw prompt and repository context;
- exact raw runner output plus expected and observed route;
- observed ownership, deliverable contracts, artifacts, visibility, catalog
  membership, phase result, and dependency outcomes;
- full created/updated diff, or explicit verified zero-write evidence for a
  blocked/refused case;
- validation results from an independent deterministic, agent, human, or E2E
  grader with a different identity and session from the runner;
- observed rationalizations;
- pass/fail result, blocker or failure codes, and notes.

A top-level pass fails when any recorded validation check is failed or blocked.
Self-attested validation and an empty diff for artifact-producing work are
decorative evidence, not a gate.

## Failure Codes

Use stable codes:

```text
migration-pending
contract-missing
contract-invalid
ownership-unresolved
phase-approval-unbound
required-artifact-missing
required-artifact-invalid
support-artifact-undeclared
eval-evidence-missing
eval-evidence-failed
dependency-write-plan-missing
dependency-output-missing
dependency-provenance-missing
dependency-role-mismatch
dependency-out-of-boundary
catalog-bundle-incomplete
catalog-phantom-entry
review-evidence-missing
primary-deliverable-incomplete
python-substitute
javascript-only
helper-only
thin-wrapper
command-first
incomplete-metadata
identity-mismatch
decorative-eval
unreferenced-helper
```

## Release Threshold

- Every deterministic fixture passes.
- Canonical, packaged, standalone, mixed-product, workflow-pack,
  validator-only, documentation-only, tracker-only, and dependency-backed
  families pass required English, terse, and Taglish GPT-5.6 Sol runs.
- Ambiguous ownership stops with zero writes.
- Authority, boundary, and dependency scenarios pass three consecutive clean
  runs when the selected task requires `pass^3`.
- A retry after grader feedback never counts as a first-attempt pass.

--- /dev/null

@@ -0,0 +1,117 @@
+---
+name: dependency-backed-skill
+description: Use when a user needs an answer grounded in the approved research fixture, with explicit source, path, SHA-256 provenance, and a clear distinction between evidence and inference.
+---
+
+# Dependency-Backed Skill
+
+## Overview
+
+Answer a fixture question using the approved dependency artifact as evidence and
+preserve provenance in the response. This skill interprets the supplied evidence;
+it does not create, refresh, promote, or overwrite dependency output, and it does
+not treat the support artifact as an independently approved primary deliverable.
+
+Invoke this workflow as `$dependency-backed-skill`.
+
+## Required Context
+
+Read the selected implementation-ready task contract and its dependency step,
+write plan, artifact record, and provenance record. Then read the approved
+support-only evidence at `artifacts/research-evidence.md`. Confirm that the task
+binds that artifact to the current deliverable and requires both `source` and
+`content_sha256` provenance. Do not substitute prior knowledge, another file, or
+an unapproved external source.
+
+## Input Contract
+
+Required inputs are:
+
+- a fixture question that can be answered or bounded by the approved evidence;
+- an implementation-ready task whose dependency step is complete;
+- the dependency artifact path, source label, and expected SHA-256 provenance;
+- a consumer deliverable matching the dependency artifact binding.
+
+An optional input may specify the desired answer format, provided it does not
+remove provenance or blur evidence and inference.
+
+Reject missing questions, incomplete dependency steps, absent artifacts,
+consumer-binding mismatches, or provenance that lacks either the source or
+content hash. A request to modify the dependency artifact, promote it to primary,
+or use an external replacement is invalid without a separately approved contract.
+
+## Workflow
+
+1. Resolve the dependency step and select only its artifact bound to this
+   deliverable with role `support` and promotion state `support-only`.
+2. Verify that the resolved path is inside the dependency write plan's expected
+   output paths and outside every protected path.
+3. Read `artifacts/research-evidence.md` without editing it.
+4. Compute the file's SHA-256 from its exact bytes and compare it with the
+   authoritative runtime provenance value. Treat the task's seeded placeholder
+   as a value that the harness reconciles to the computed hash, never as the
+   literal evidence hash.
+5. Extract only claims supported by the artifact. Label any necessary deduction
+   as inference and explain its connection to the evidence.
+6. Answer the fixture question concisely. If the evidence is insufficient, say
+   what cannot be established instead of filling the gap from general knowledge.
+7. Append a provenance block containing the dependency project, selected skill,
+   source, artifact path, computed `content_sha256`, artifact role, and promotion
+   state.
+8. Report whether the dependency reconciled successfully and hand the answer to
+   the caller without changing task phase, catalog state, or dependency files.
+
+## Decision Points
+
+| Condition | Action |
+| --- | --- |
+| Artifact, binding, and provenance agree | Produce an evidence-backed answer. |
+| Evidence supports only part of the question | Answer the supported portion and state the limitation. |
+| Computed hash differs from authoritative provenance | Stop; do not quote or summarize the artifact. |
+| Source or hash provenance is missing | Stop with `dependency-provenance-missing`. |
+| Artifact is absent | Stop with `dependency-output-missing`. |
+| Role, promotion state, or consumer binding differs | Stop with `dependency-role-mismatch`. |
+| Path escapes the approved output or touches a protected path | Stop with `dependency-out-of-boundary`. |
+
+Default deny applies: an unrecognized artifact, source, write request, or
+promotion request cannot be inferred as approved.
+
+## Failure Handling
+
+On failure, emit the stable blocker code, the observed path or provenance field,
+and the recovery owner. The lifecycle owner must repair task metadata or approval;
+the dependency owner must restore or regenerate missing or mismatched evidence.
+Do not repair metadata, rewrite evidence, weaken the hash check, search the
+network, or silently answer from memory. A blocked run produces no file writes.
+
+## Human Boundaries
+
+The user or outer lifecycle harness retains approval of dependency execution,
+external writes, artifact promotion, source changes, publication, and task-phase
+transitions. This skill may read the approved support artifact and produce an
+answer only. Consequential claims and any decision based on incomplete evidence
+remain subject to human review.
+
+## Output Contract
+
+Return:
+
+- the evidence-backed answer, with inference and limitations explicitly marked;
+- dependency reconciliation status;
+- provenance fields for `dependency_project`, `selected_skill`, `source`,
+  `path`, `content_sha256`, `artifact_role`, and `promotion_state`;
+- any stable blocker code and recovery owner;
+- a handoff to the invoking lifecycle task.
+
+The workflow creates no support files and never alters the approved evidence.
+
+## Completion Gate
+
+Complete only when the question is addressed solely within the evidence's scope,
+the exact artifact is bound to the consumer, its computed SHA-256 matches runtime
+provenance, source/path/role/promotion provenance is present, evidence and
+inference are distinguishable, and no protected or external write occurred.
+The outer harness owns deterministic fixtures, GPT-5.6 Sol fresh-agent runs,
+security checks, and Standards-plus-Spec review; their required results must pass
+before release even though this inner construction step does not run them.
+

@@ -0,0 +1,140 @@
+---
+name: audit-project-state
+description: Audit repository policy, task state, working-tree changes, and approved edit boundaries before modifying a project. Use when Codex is about to implement, fix, refactor, or otherwise write files in an existing project.
+---
+
+# Audit Project State
+
+## Overview
+
+Establish a read-only, evidence-backed pre-edit baseline. Identify the active
+request, applicable repository instructions, current work, approved write
+scope, protected paths, and verification expectations before any mutation.
+Return either a bounded edit plan or a stable blocker.
+
+This skill does not edit files, repair task metadata, discard changes, invoke
+other workflows, obtain approvals, or run destructive commands. It audits the
+state that a later implementation workflow may use.
+
+## Required Context
+
+Start with the operator's current request and the repository root. Discover and
+read every policy file that applies to the intended target, from root policy to
+the nearest nested policy. Read the selected task or specification when one is
+named, together with directly referenced acceptance criteria and artifact
+bindings. Inspect repository status and the candidate target files without
+changing them.
+
+When available and relevant, inspect project manifests, existing tests,
+generated-file notices, ownership declarations, and nearby prior art. Load only
+sources needed to establish the edit boundary and verification plan; never
+search external systems unless the operator explicitly placed them in scope.
+
+## Input Contract
+
+Required inputs are:
+
+- a repository or project root;
+- a concrete user intent or selected implementation task;
+- at least one candidate target, component, or deliverable to audit.
+
+Optional inputs are an explicit allowed-path list, protected paths, task-state
+locator, desired verification commands, and a known baseline revision.
+
+Treat inputs as invalid when the request conflicts with applicable policy, the
+selected task cannot be identified uniquely, approved paths disagree with the
+requested deliverable, the target is outside the repository boundary, or a
+write plan depends on unavailable external authority. Multiple candidate tasks
+are not permission to choose one silently.
+
+## Workflow
+
+1. Resolve the repository root and restate the requested outcome without
+   broadening it.
+2. Locate applicable policy files along the path from the root to each proposed
+   target. Record precedence and extract write, test, ownership, and approval
+   constraints.
+3. Resolve the selected task or specification. Record its status, acceptance
+   criteria, artifact identities, approved phases, dependencies, and explicit
+   exclusions. Do not repair missing state.
+4. Inspect the working tree with read-only status and diff operations. Separate
+   pre-existing modified, staged, untracked, and ignored files from proposed
+   work, and attribute nothing to the current request without evidence.
+5. Inspect candidate targets and nearby implementation patterns. Identify
+   generated files, ownership boundaries, existing identities, and tests that
+   constrain the change.
+6. Reconcile requested work, policy, task approvals, and current changes into
+   an exact allowed-path set. Record protected and unrelated paths separately.
+7. Define proportionate local verification for the proposed edit, including
+   the narrowest relevant tests and any required broader checks. Do not execute
+   mutating setup as part of this audit.
+8. Emit the audit report. If all required facts agree, provide a bounded edit
+   plan; otherwise stop with the applicable blocker and the evidence needed to
+   recover.
+
+## Decision Points
+
+| Condition | Result |
+| --- | --- |
+| Policy, task, ownership, and paths agree | `ready` with an exact edit boundary |
+| Existing changes are unrelated but separable | `ready-with-cautions`; preserve them |
+| Existing changes overlap a target and intent is clear | `ready-with-cautions`; require preservation and focused diff review |
+| Task, target, ownership, or approval is ambiguous | `blocked`; request the missing decision |
+| Policy forbids the requested edit | `blocked`; cite the controlling constraint |
+| Target is generated | `blocked` until its declared source and regeneration path are in scope |
+| Required evidence cannot be read safely | `blocked`; make no writes |
+
+Default deny applies: absence of a constraint or approval is not approval to
+invent one, expand scope, modify external state, or overwrite existing work.
+
+## Failure Handling
+
+Use stable blocker codes: `repository-root-unresolved`, `policy-conflict`,
+`task-unresolved`, `task-state-invalid`, `ownership-unresolved`,
+`approval-missing`, `edit-boundary-unresolved`, `target-protected`,
+`generated-source-unresolved`, `working-tree-overlap`, and
+`verification-unresolved`.
+
+For a blocker, report the observed evidence, the smallest missing fact or
+approval, and who can supply it. Stop the audit with zero writes. Never clean
+the tree, reset files, edit task state, weaken policy, or reinterpret an
+unreadable source as permission.
+
+## Human Boundaries
+
+The operator retains decisions that broaden scope, select among ambiguous
+tasks, authorize protected or external writes, resolve ownership conflicts,
+discard or overwrite existing changes, change task phase, and waive required
+verification. The later implementation workflow—not this audit—owns edits and
+commands that can mutate project state.
+
+## Output Contract
+
+Return a structured report containing:
+
+- status: `ready`, `ready-with-cautions`, or `blocked`;
+- resolved repository root, user intent, and selected task or specification;
+- applicable policies with their scopes and controlling constraints;
+- working-tree baseline, including staged, modified, and untracked paths;
+- exact allowed, protected, unrelated, and overlapping path sets;
+- ownership, generated-source, dependency, and approval findings;
+- proposed edit sequence and verification plan when ready;
+- blocker codes, evidence, recovery owner, and required next decision when
+  blocked.
+
+The report is the handoff to the implementation workflow. It must distinguish
+observations from inferences and must not claim that any edit or test ran.
+
+## Completion Gate
+
+Complete only when the applicable policy chain and selected task have been
+resolved, the working-tree baseline is captured, candidate targets and
+ownership are inspected, allowed and protected paths are explicit, and the
+verification plan is tied to acceptance criteria. Confirm the audit performed
+no writes and proposed no destructive action.
+
+For a ready result, require internally consistent identity, approval, and path
+evidence plus a reviewable edit plan. For a blocked result, require a stable
+code and actionable recovery owner. Any required security, deterministic,
+model-run, or human-review gate remains pending for the invoking lifecycle or
+implementation workflow; this skill never self-attests those gates.

index 0000000..5b2d5bf
--- /dev/null
+++ b/.agents/skills/audit-project-state/SKILL.md
@@ -0,0 +1,152 @@
+---
+name: audit-project-state
+description: Audit a project's policies, task tracker, artifacts, dependencies, approvals, and repository evidence for consistency and readiness without changing state. Use when a user asks to inspect project status, find workflow drift or blockers, verify that task and artifact claims match the repository, or produce an evidence-backed readiness report.
+---
+
+# Audit Project State
+
+## Overview
+
+Produce a read-only, evidence-backed account of a project's current workflow
+state. Reconcile policy, tracker records, approvals, deliverable contracts,
+dependencies, and files on disk; distinguish verified facts from inferences;
+and identify the next safe owner action.
+
+This skill does not implement deliverables, repair tracker data, transition a
+phase, publish artifacts, approve work, or treat narrative claims as proof.
+
+## Required Context
+
+Before drawing conclusions, locate and read:
+
+- the repository's root policy and any policy governing the selected project;
+- the project state, task index, and every non-done task relevant to the audit;
+- the selected task, including its phase, approvals, contracts, artifact
+  bindings, dependencies, completion conditions, and recorded evidence;
+- the actual files and version-control state for every claimed artifact; and
+- prior audit, validation, review, or model-run evidence referenced by the
+  selected task.
+
+Use repository-local sources as authoritative unless policy explicitly names
+an external system of record. Note every required source that is absent or
+unreadable. Do not silently replace missing evidence with convention or memory.
+
+## Input Contract
+
+Require a project or repository scope and an audit question such as status,
+consistency, readiness, blockers, or next action. When a task is named, resolve
+exactly one task identity. When no task is named, audit project-level state and
+all non-done tasks that can affect the requested conclusion.
+
+Optional inputs are a phase or deliverable filter, a requested evidence cutoff,
+and a user-supplied claim to verify. Filters narrow reporting only; they do not
+permit ignoring dependencies or policy that affect the conclusion.
+
+Reject or stop narrowing when project or task identity is ambiguous. Treat
+conflicting phase records, unbound approvals, multiple artifacts claiming one
+exclusive identity, and completion claims without inspectable evidence as
+inconsistencies rather than choosing a convenient interpretation.
+
+## Workflow
+
+1. Establish the repository root, project boundary, governing policies, audit
+   scope, and evidence cutoff. Record the exact sources inspected.
+2. Inventory project state and relevant tasks. Normalize each task's identity,
+   status, phase, owner, next action, contracts, approvals, dependencies, and
+   linked artifacts without editing the source records.
+3. Trace every selected deliverable from contract to approval, artifact
+   binding, on-disk path, and completion evidence. Inspect content where the
+   claim depends on content; file existence alone does not prove validity.
+4. Reconcile dependency edges in both directions. Verify that prerequisites
+   have the required outcome and provenance, and flag missing, stale, cyclic,
+   role-mismatched, or out-of-boundary dependencies.
+5. Compare recorded state with repository evidence, including missing files,
+   unexpected files in protected or approved boundaries, duplicate identities,
+   dirty changes, and claims whose evidence predates the current artifact.
+6. Evaluate each requested readiness or completion condition independently.
+   Label it `verified`, `failed`, or `unknown`, and attach the source or missing
+   evidence that justifies that label.
+7. Classify findings by impact, identify the recovery owner, and derive the
+   smallest safe next action. Keep observed facts, policy interpretations, and
+   inferences visibly separate.
+8. Return the audit report and stop. Do not mutate files, trackers, approvals,
+   catalogs, external systems, or task phases as part of the audit.
+
+## Decision Points
+
+| Condition | Result |
+| --- | --- |
+| Governing policy and identity resolve uniquely | Continue with the bounded audit |
+| Required source is missing or unreadable | Mark affected conclusions `unknown` |
+| Tracker and repository agree with current evidence | Mark the checked claim `verified` |
+| Sources conflict | Report the conflict; do not select a winner without policy authority |
+| Artifact exists but validation or review evidence is absent or stale | Do not report readiness or completion |
+| Requested action would write or approve state | Stop at a proposed action and name the authorized owner |
+
+Default deny applies to readiness: an unverified condition cannot be promoted
+to `verified`. A project's overall result is `blocked` when a required condition
+fails, `indeterminate` when a required condition is unknown, and `ready` only
+when every required condition is verified with current evidence.
+
+## Failure Handling
+
+Use stable blocker codes in findings:
+
+- `audit-scope-ambiguous` for unresolved project or task identity;
+- `audit-policy-missing` for absent governing policy;
+- `audit-source-unreadable` for required evidence that cannot be inspected;
+- `audit-state-conflict` for contradictory authoritative records;
+- `audit-artifact-missing` or `audit-artifact-mismatch` for binding failures;
+- `audit-approval-unbound` for approval that does not bind phase, deliverable,
+  artifact, and role as required;
+- `audit-dependency-unresolved` for incomplete or invalid dependency outcomes;
+- `audit-evidence-missing` or `audit-evidence-stale` for unsupported claims; and
+- `audit-boundary-violation` for observed state outside an authorized boundary.
+
+Stop the affected conclusion, preserve the observed evidence, and name the
+policy owner, task owner, artifact owner, or operator who can recover it. Never
+repair evidence, rewrite history, weaken a gate, or convert `unknown` to a pass.
+
+## Human Boundaries
+
+The user or designated workflow owner retains authority to resolve ambiguous
+scope, interpret disputed policy, accept risk, approve contracts and paths,
+change task or phase state, authorize external access, publish artifacts, and
+perform consequential dependency actions. Ask before expanding the audit into
+external systems or unrelated projects.
+
+Read-only repository inspection and local non-mutating queries are permitted.
+Do not run commands that generate, format, install, migrate, validate by
+mutation, or otherwise alter project state under the label of an audit.
+
+## Output Contract
+
+Return:
+
+1. audit scope, evidence cutoff, and governing policies;
+2. an overall result of `ready`, `blocked`, or `indeterminate`;
+3. a concise state summary for each in-scope task and deliverable;
+4. a condition matrix with `verified`, `failed`, or `unknown`, plus exact
+   repository paths or record identifiers supporting each result;
+5. findings with severity, stable blocker code, observed fact, impact, and
+   recovery owner;
+6. dependency and approval reconciliation;
+7. noted dirty or untracked state relevant to the conclusions; and
+8. the smallest safe next action, clearly identified as advice rather than an
+   executed transition.
+
+If the user requests machine-readable output, preserve these fields and keep
+unknown values explicit. Do not claim a file was created or state was changed.
+
+## Completion Gate
+
+The audit is complete only when every in-scope task and deliverable has been
+traced to its authoritative records and current repository evidence; every
+required condition has a supported tri-state result; dependencies, approvals,
+boundaries, and artifact identities have been reconciled; conflicts and stale
+evidence are disclosed; and every blocker has an owner and safe recovery step.
+
+Before returning, check that no files or external state were changed by the
+audit, every factual claim cites an inspected source, no unknown was reported
+as success, security-sensitive content is summarized without leaking secrets,
+and the overall result follows the default-deny readiness rule.

@@ -0,0 +1,4 @@
+interface:

--- /dev/null
+++ b/.agents/skills/audit-project-state/SKILL.md
@@ -0,0 +1,167 @@
+---
+name: audit-project-state
+description: Use before editing a Foundry project to audit repository policy, task readiness, ownership, approvals, dependencies, and the current worktree, then produce an edit/no-edit decision and a bounded implementation plan.
+---
+
+# Audit Project State
+
+## Overview
+
+Audit the current Foundry project before any edit. Establish what work is
+authorized, which files and owners are in scope, whether the repository is safe
+to change, and what evidence the implementation must preserve. Return a
+default-deny `proceed`, `proceed-with-constraints`, or `stop` decision plus a
+bounded edit plan.
+
+This skill is read-only. It does not edit files, repair task state, approve
+contracts, resolve product decisions, publish artifacts, or run consequential
+dependency actions. Invoke it as `$audit-project-state` before handing an
+approved plan to an implementation skill.
+
+## Required Context
+
+Load, in repository precedence order:
+
+1. Root and target-directory policy files, including every applicable
+   `AGENTS.md`.
+2. The selected task or explicit user request, including its current phase,
+   acceptance criteria, approvals, artifact bindings, and completion gates.
+3. Project state and every non-done task that can constrain or conflict with
+   the selected work.
+4. The current worktree status and the existing contents and identities at each
+   proposed target path.
+5. Referenced source artifacts, prior art, dependency plans, and validation
+   requirements that are necessary to decide whether editing is safe.
+
+Read only the context authorized by repository policy. Record missing inputs;
+do not invent their contents or treat an unverified path as absent.
+
+## Input Contract
+
+Require:
+
+- one repository or project boundary;
+- one selected task or a concrete requested change;
+- the proposed operation (`create`, `update`, or `delete`) and target paths, if
+  already known; and
+- access to applicable policy and local project state.
+
+Accept optional candidate artifacts, dependency steps, validation commands,
+and an operator-supplied scope restriction. Optional inputs may narrow scope
+but may not override policy or supply missing approval.
+
+Inputs are invalid when they select multiple competing tasks without priority,
+combine contradictory operations for one target, request paths outside the
+declared boundary, claim an approval without an artifact/role/path binding, or
+require unavailable context to establish ownership. On invalid input, stop
+without proposing edits.
+
+## Workflow
+
+1. Identify the repository root, target project, selected task, requested
+   operation, and candidate paths. Preserve the operator's explicit scope.
+2. Resolve all applicable policy files from root to target. Extract protected
+   paths, permitted reads and writes, required tools, approval rules, and
+   validation ownership. Resolve conflicts in favor of the more specific policy
+   unless a higher-level policy explicitly forbids override.
+3. Inspect task readiness. Confirm the selected action matches the current
+   phase and that each proposed artifact is bound to the correct deliverable,
+   role, path, and approval. Distinguish implementation authority from review,
+   publication, and tracker authority.
+4. Inspect existing targets and nearby project structure. For an update or
+   delete, resolve exactly one existing identity. For a create, detect collisions
+   and aliases. Never reinterpret an update as a create.
+5. Inspect the worktree without changing it. Attribute relevant existing edits
+   where possible, flag overlap with candidate paths, and preserve unrelated
+   user changes. Do not require a clean worktree when non-overlapping changes
+   can be safely isolated.
+6. Reconcile dependencies. For every dependency-producing step, require its
+   owner, inputs, outputs, destination, provenance, and approval. Separate safe
+   read-only discovery from consequential writes or external actions.
+7. Trace each acceptance criterion to a proposed artifact and verification
+   method. Identify required deterministic checks, model evaluations, security
+   checks, and human review, while respecting which actor owns each gate.
+8. Classify every candidate path as approved, constrained, protected,
+   unrelated, or unresolved. Reduce the plan to the smallest complete set of
+   authorized edits; do not add speculative support files.
+9. Emit the audit report and decision. Handoff only an independently
+   inspectable plan; never perform the edits from this skill.
+
+## Decision Points
+
+| Condition | Decision | Required treatment |
+| --- | --- | --- |
+| Policies, task, ownership, approvals, targets, and dependencies agree | `proceed` | Provide the exact bounded plan and gates |
+| Work is authorized but safe execution requires explicit path or sequencing constraints | `proceed-with-constraints` | State each constraint and exclude all other writes |
+| Ownership is unresolved, approval is missing, policy conflicts, identity is ambiguous, or a protected path is required | `stop` | Make no edit plan beyond recovery actions |
+
+Use default-deny behavior: uncertainty about authority, identity, or boundary is
+a reason to stop, not permission to infer intent. Existing unrelated worktree
+changes alone are not a blocker; overlap that cannot be safely separated is.
+
+## Failure Handling
+
+Return stable blocker codes with observed evidence and a recovery owner:
+
+- `audit-context-missing`: required policy, task, or project state is absent;
+- `audit-input-invalid`: inputs conflict or do not identify one auditable change;
+- `audit-phase-not-ready`: the current task phase does not authorize editing;
+- `audit-approval-unbound`: approval is missing or not bound to artifact and path;
+- `audit-ownership-unresolved`: no single owner or target surface is authoritative;
+- `audit-identity-ambiguous`: update/delete resolves zero or multiple identities;
+- `audit-boundary-violation`: a requested path or action exceeds the boundary;
+- `audit-worktree-overlap`: existing changes overlap and cannot be preserved;
+- `audit-dependency-plan-missing`: a dependency step lacks a complete write plan;
+- `audit-validation-unresolved`: required completion evidence has no valid owner.
+
+For every blocker, cite the relevant local fact, say what is still unknown, and
+name who can resolve it. Do not repair state, weaken acceptance criteria, or
+convert a stopped audit into a conditional pass.
+
+## Human Boundaries
+
+The operator retains approval of scope, contracts, ownership, destructive or
+external actions, protected-path changes, publication, phase transitions, and
+acceptance of residual risk. The lifecycle harness retains tracker mutation and
+the gates assigned to it. Implementation and review skills retain their own
+edits and judgments.
+
+This skill may inspect authorized local state and recommend recovery. It must
+not write files, install dependencies, contact external systems, start nested
+agents or model runs, grant approvals, or claim another actor's validation.
+
+## Output Contract
+
+Return one structured audit report containing:
+
+- repository, project, selected task, requested operation, and audit timestamp;
+- applicable policies and the precedence used;
+- current phase and approval/binding findings;
+- target identity, ownership boundary, visibility, and runtime targets;
+- candidate-path classifications and protected/unrelated paths;
+- relevant worktree findings, with existing changes kept distinct;
+- dependency provenance and unresolved dependency actions;
+- acceptance-criterion-to-artifact and artifact-to-validation mappings;
+- decision: `proceed`, `proceed-with-constraints`, or `stop`;
+- exact allowed edits and ordered implementation steps when proceeding;
+- forbidden writes, retained human actions, blocker codes, recovery owners, and
+  the next skill or lifecycle owner for handoff.
+
+Claims must point to inspected state. Mark unavailable evidence as unavailable;
+do not substitute narrative assurance.
+
+## Completion Gate
+
+The audit is complete only when all required context was inspected; one task
+and operation were resolved; applicable policy precedence is explicit; every
+candidate path has a classification; ownership, approvals, identity,
+dependencies, and worktree overlap were checked; and each acceptance criterion
+has an evidence owner.
+
+For a proceeding decision, the plan must be deterministic, boundary-safe,
+minimal, and independently reviewable, with required deterministic checks,
+model-run evaluations, security validation, and human review assigned to their
+proper owners. A `stop` decision is complete only when it reports stable
+blockers and concrete recovery actions. Completion of this audit never means
+that implementation, validation, review, publication, or lifecycle work has
+also completed.

+---
+name: health-workflow
+description: Use when work in the Health project needs to be scoped, implemented, or reviewed while preserving clinical-safety boundaries, evidence provenance, privacy, and explicit human approval for consequential health decisions.
+---
+
+# Health Workflow
+
+## Overview
+
+Turn an approved Health project request into a bounded, reviewable deliverable.
+The workflow separates product and engineering work from medical judgment,
+preserves the provenance of health claims, and keeps sensitive data out of
+artifacts unless its use is explicitly authorized.
+
+This skill does not diagnose, prescribe, provide emergency guidance, approve a
+clinical claim, or authorize deployment. It is packaged with the Health project
+and is not an active Foundry skill.
+
+## Required Context
+
+Before changing files, load the repository and Health project policies, the
+selected task and its acceptance criteria, the approved artifact paths, and the
+current contents of every file that may be edited. Load source material named
+by the task, including its version or publication date, and inspect relevant
+tests and prior project patterns.
+
+Treat clinical, legal, privacy, accessibility, and security review records as
+inputs only when they are present in the approved project context. Do not infer
+an approval from silence or from earlier work on a similar feature.
+
+## Input Contract
+
+Required inputs are one selected Health project task, an explicit requested
+outcome, acceptance criteria, approved write paths, and the governing project
+policy. Any health claim must also identify its source and intended audience.
+
+Optional inputs include designs, test fixtures, de-identified sample data,
+approved terminology, and existing review evidence. Patient or member data may
+be used only when the task and policy explicitly authorize that exact use.
+
+Reject inputs that combine implementation with an unapproved clinical decision,
+request writes outside the approved boundary, rely on unattributed health
+claims, expose secrets or identifiable health data, or give conflicting
+instructions about audience, jurisdiction, or risk classification.
+
+## Workflow
+
+1. Restate the requested outcome, audience, acceptance criteria, approved paths,
+   and explicit non-goals from the selected task.
+2. Classify each requested change as product behavior, engineering behavior,
+   health content, data handling, or a combination. Record which categories
+   require a human reviewer under project policy.
+3. Trace every health claim to the supplied authoritative source. Preserve
+   qualifications, population limits, dates, and uncertainty; do not strengthen
+   a source's conclusion.
+4. Minimize data use. Prefer synthetic or de-identified fixtures and prevent
+   sensitive values from entering source, logs, snapshots, examples, or output.
+5. Inspect existing project conventions and implement the smallest complete
+   change inside the approved paths. Keep safety notices and escalation paths
+   visible and testable when the task requires them.
+6. Verify acceptance criteria with the repository's approved local checks.
+   Inspect the final diff for unsupported claims, sensitive data, misleading
+   certainty, boundary violations, and unrelated edits.
+7. Produce the output contract and identify every still-required clinical,
+   privacy, security, accessibility, legal, product, or deployment approval.
+
+## Decision Points
+
+| Condition | Action |
+| --- | --- |
+| Pure product or engineering change with no health claim or sensitive data | Implement within the approved paths and run local checks. |
+| Health claim has an approved, applicable source | Preserve its scope and provenance, then require the designated content or clinical review. |
+| Claim source, audience, jurisdiction, or applicability is missing | Stop the affected work with `health-evidence-missing`. |
+| Identifiable health data is requested without exact authorization | Stop with `health-data-authorization-missing`; do not copy or transform the data. |
+| Request could influence diagnosis, treatment, triage, or emergency action | Do not make the decision; require an authorized clinical owner. |
+| Instructions or approvals conflict | Default deny and report `health-contract-conflict`. |
+
+When risk is uncertain, select the more restrictive branch and preserve a clear
+recovery path rather than silently narrowing the task.
+
+## Failure Handling
+
+Stop before the affected write for `health-evidence-missing`,
+`health-data-authorization-missing`, `health-clinical-approval-required`,
+`health-contract-conflict`, `health-boundary-violation`, or
+`health-validation-failed`. Report the observed fact, impacted acceptance
+criterion, files left unchanged, and the role that can resolve the blocker.
+
+Never replace missing evidence with general model knowledge, weaken a safety
+requirement to make a check pass, or include sensitive values as diagnostic
+evidence. Preserve safe completed work only when it is independently valid and
+clearly separated from the blocked portion.
+
+## Human Boundaries
+
+Authorized humans retain clinical judgment, diagnosis and treatment decisions,
+approval of health claims, privacy and legal determinations, production access,
+deployment, and any action affecting a person's care or benefits. The user also
+retains approval of scope changes, new dependencies, external writes, and use of
+real health data.
+
+This workflow may inspect and edit only task-approved project files and may run
+only non-consequential local verification. It must not contact patients,
+providers, regulators, or external systems.
+
+## Output Contract
+
+Return the task identity and outcome; created or modified files; acceptance
+criteria mapped to concrete implementation evidence; checks run and their
+results; health-claim sources and applicability limits; data classification and
+data-minimization notes; unresolved blocker codes; and required human approvals.
+Explicitly state that this bundle remains project-local and inactive in the
+Foundry catalog.
+
+## Completion Gate
+
+Complete only when the requested artifacts exist at approved paths, acceptance
+criteria have inspectable evidence, local deterministic checks pass, the final
+diff contains no unrelated changes or sensitive data, and every health claim is
+traceable without overstating its source. All reviews required by project policy
+must be recorded with no unresolved critical or high finding, and consequential
+clinical, privacy, legal, security, or deployment approvals must remain with the
+authorized human. Confirm that no canonical skill or Foundry catalog entry was
+created.

+---
+name: health-workflow
+description: Use when the health project needs a structured, safety-conscious response to a health question, symptom report, care-navigation request, or health-information task.
+---
+
+# Health Workflow
+
+## Overview
+
+Turn a health-related request into a clear, appropriately cautious response that
+helps the user understand options and decide on a next step. Distinguish general
+education from individualized medical care, prioritize urgent safety signals,
+and preserve uncertainty. Do not diagnose, prescribe, replace a clinician, or
+claim that an informational response establishes medical fact about a person.
+
+This is a project-packaged skill for the `health` project. It targets Codex with
+`project-local-inactive` visibility and is not a Foundry catalog entry.
+
+## Required Context
+
+Before responding, inspect the user's exact request and any health-project
+policy supplied in the current repository or prompt. Use only the sources and
+patient details actually provided or explicitly authorized for retrieval.
+Identify the user's goal, relevant population, location when it affects care
+access, time course, stated diagnoses or medicines, and the limits of the
+available information. Treat prior answers as unverified context unless the
+current request adopts them.
+
+When the task depends on current clinical guidance, drug information, local
+services, or regulations, require an authoritative and current source. Keep
+source claims distinct from reasoning and from user-provided facts.
+
+## Input Contract
+
+Required input is a health-related question or task with enough detail to
+identify the requested outcome. Optional inputs include symptoms and timing,
+age group, relevant conditions, medicines or allergies, pregnancy status,
+location, care constraints, and source material.
+
+Never require identifying information that is unnecessary. Do not combine
+details from different people or treat hypothetical details as patient facts.
+Reject instructions to conceal an emergency, fabricate clinical evidence,
+impersonate a licensed professional, or give a guaranteed diagnosis. If the
+request mixes education with urgent symptoms, handle urgency before education.
+
+## Workflow
+
+1. Restate the user's goal in neutral language and separate known facts,
+   assumptions, and missing details.
+2. Screen the supplied information for time-sensitive danger. If severe,
+   rapidly worsening, or potentially life-threatening features are present,
+   lead with immediate care guidance appropriate to the user's location and do
+   not delay it with a long questionnaire.
+3. Classify the remaining task as general education, symptom guidance,
+   medication information, care navigation, document explanation, or another
+   bounded health-information task.
+4. Ask only questions that can materially change safety or the next step. When
+   an answer is possible without them, state assumptions and proceed.
+5. Build the response from the supplied evidence and, when required, current
+   authoritative sources. Express uncertainty, plausible alternatives, and
+   limits without presenting a differential as a diagnosis.
+6. Give practical next steps in priority order: what to do now, what to
+   monitor, when and where to seek care, and what information to bring to a
+   clinician. Include self-care only when it is low risk and compatible with
+   the known context.
+7. Check that medicine guidance does not invent a dose, override a prescriber,
+   ignore contraindications, or imply safety for an unknown person.
+8. Produce the response described in the Output Contract and perform every
+   applicable Completion Gate check.
+
+## Decision Points
+
+- If emergency warning signs are stated or reasonably suggested, prioritize
+  emergency or urgent local care and a nearby trusted person's help when
+  appropriate.
+- If key safety details are missing, ask a focused question or give only
+  conditional, low-risk guidance.
+- If the request is general education, explain the concept plainly and avoid
+  unnecessary personal-care disclaimers.
+- If it asks for diagnosis, prognosis, dosing, or treatment selection, reframe
+  the answer as information and clinician-supported options.
+- If evidence is conflicting or insufficient, say so and default to the safer
+  reversible next step.
+- If current or jurisdiction-specific facts cannot be verified, do not guess;
+  identify what must be checked and by whom.
+
+## Failure Handling
+
+Stop or narrow the response when safe completion is not possible. Use stable
+blockers: `urgent-care-required` for time-sensitive escalation,
+`insufficient-safety-context` for missing facts that prevent safe guidance,
+`authoritative-source-required` for unverifiable current claims, and
+`request-out-of-scope` for diagnosis, prescribing, deception, or another
+disallowed outcome. State the observed reason, the safest available next step,
+and who can resolve it. Never fill gaps with invented patient facts or sources.
+
+## Human Boundaries
+
+The user decides whether to share optional health details and whether to seek
+care. Licensed clinicians retain diagnosis, treatment, prescribing, and
+patient-specific interpretation. Emergency services and local professionals
+retain triage authority. Obtain explicit user approval before any external
+message, appointment, purchase, record change, or disclosure; this workflow
+does not perform those actions by itself.
+
+## Output Contract
+
+Return a concise response containing: the understood goal; urgent action first
+when applicable; an evidence-calibrated explanation; prioritized next steps;
+specific escalation or monitoring criteria; and clearly labeled assumptions,
+unknowns, and sources when used. For a blocked task, return the stable blocker,
+observed evidence, safe recovery step, and responsible human or service.
+
+Do not create files, modify health records, publish claims, or add this packaged
+skill to the Foundry catalog. The handoff is to the user or the appropriate
+health professional, not an automated consequential action.
+
+## Completion Gate
+
+Complete only when the response addresses the stated goal, puts urgent safety
+before detail, distinguishes information from individualized care, preserves
+uncertainty, contains no fabricated facts or sources, and gives an actionable
+handoff. Confirm privacy minimization, medicine safety, and jurisdiction limits
+where applicable. Any required current-source check, project validation,
+independent model evaluation, security review, or human clinical review must be
+performed by the invoking harness or authorized reviewer; if required evidence
+is absent, report the applicable blocker rather than claiming completion.

@@ -0,0 +1,133 @@
+---
+name: health-workflow
+description: Use when a Health package request needs a structured, safety-conscious health information workflow that separates general education from urgent, diagnostic, prescribing, or other clinician-owned decisions.
+---
+
+# Health Workflow
+
+## Overview
+
+Turn a Health package request into a clear, evidence-aware response or handoff.
+Clarify the goal, screen for urgency, distinguish general information from
+individual medical advice, and give practical next steps at an appropriate
+level of certainty. This skill does not diagnose, prescribe, replace a
+clinician, or activate or publish anything in the Foundry catalog. It is a
+project-packaged workflow with `project-local-inactive` visibility for the
+Health project and Codex runtime only.
+
+## Required Context
+
+Read the user's complete request and any supplied symptoms, diagnoses,
+medications, measurements, test results, constraints, location, and preferred
+language. Load applicable Health project policy and the source material the
+user explicitly provides. When the task depends on current clinical guidance,
+drug information, local emergency resources, or another time-sensitive fact,
+use only approved authoritative sources and retain enough provenance to cite
+them. Treat missing personal context as unknown; do not infer it from tone,
+identity, or unrelated history.
+
+## Input Contract
+
+Require a health-related question or goal. For individualized guidance, seek
+the minimum relevant context: age group, main concern, onset and duration,
+severity and trend, relevant conditions, medicines or allergies, pregnancy
+status when pertinent, and country or region when care access matters. All are
+optional when unnecessary or when the user declines to provide them.
+
+Do not combine incompatible instructions: a request for certainty with
+insufficient evidence, a demand for diagnosis or prescription without a
+qualified clinician, or a request to ignore urgent warning signs. Do not treat
+an image, wearable reading, isolated laboratory value, or symptom list as a
+standalone diagnosis. Minimize sensitive data and never ask for names, exact
+addresses, account credentials, or medical-record identifiers.
+
+## Workflow
+
+1. Restate the user's goal in neutral language and identify whether they want
+   education, options, preparation for care, help interpreting supplied
+   information, or immediate triage.
+2. Screen the supplied facts for time-sensitive warning signs before offering
+   routine guidance. If danger may be immediate, lead with the urgent action
+   and location-appropriate emergency or poison-control direction.
+3. Ask only the clarifying questions that could materially change safety or
+   the answer. If the user cannot answer, proceed with clearly labeled limits.
+4. Separate known facts, reasonable possibilities, and unknowns. Explain
+   relevant concepts in plain language and avoid presenting a differential as
+   a diagnosis.
+5. Offer proportionate next steps: low-risk self-care or monitoring when
+   suitable, what to record, when and where to seek professional care, and
+   what questions to bring to a clinician or pharmacist.
+6. For medicines, discuss general use and safety only from authoritative
+   information; check for relevant contraindication or interaction context and
+   defer starting, stopping, or changing prescription treatment to a qualified
+   professional.
+7. Cite consulted sources near the claims they support, state uncertainty and
+   evidence limits, and answer in the user's preferred language when clear.
+8. End with a concise action summary and explicit escalation conditions. Do
+   not write records, contact providers, book care, or disclose information.
+
+## Decision Points
+
+| Situation | Route |
+| --- | --- |
+| Immediate danger or severe warning signs | Stop routine guidance; direct urgent local help |
+| Possible medication poisoning, overdose, or dangerous interaction | Direct emergency or poison-control help; do not suggest home reversal |
+| Individual diagnosis, prescription, or dose change requested | Give bounded education and refer the decision to a clinician or pharmacist |
+| Current or local facts materially affect the answer | Consult approved authoritative sources or state that verification is unavailable |
+| General, low-risk education with adequate context | Explain options, monitoring, and escalation signs |
+| Insufficient context with meaningful safety impact | Ask focused questions; otherwise default-deny the unsafe recommendation |
+
+When multiple routes apply, use the most safety-protective route. A lack of
+evidence never becomes evidence that an option is safe.
+
+## Failure Handling
+
+Stop or narrow the response with a stable blocker when needed:
+
+- `urgent-care-required`: warning signs make routine guidance inappropriate;
+- `clinical-authority-required`: the requested diagnosis, prescription, or
+  consequential treatment decision belongs to a qualified professional;
+- `health-context-insufficient`: missing information prevents a safe answer;
+- `authoritative-source-unavailable`: a time-sensitive claim cannot be
+  verified through an approved source;
+- `privacy-boundary`: the request requires unnecessary sensitive data or an
+  unauthorized disclosure.
+
+State the observed reason, give the safest available recovery step, and name
+the recovery owner: the user for optional context, an appropriate clinician or
+pharmacist for clinical decisions, emergency services or poison control for
+urgent risk, and the Health package owner for policy or tooling gaps. Never
+fill a gap with fabricated certainty.
+
+## Human Boundaries
+
+The user decides whether to share optional health details and whether to seek
+care. Licensed professionals retain diagnosis, prescribing, treatment changes,
+and interpretation that requires a clinical relationship. The user or an
+authorized operator retains consent for records, communications, bookings,
+purchases, and disclosures. This workflow must not perform those actions or
+claim professional authority. Health project owners retain approval for
+package changes; Foundry owners retain activation, catalog promotion, and
+publishing authority.
+
+## Output Contract
+
+Return a response containing: the interpreted goal; any urgent disposition;
+the relevant facts and declared unknowns; a bounded explanation; practical
+next steps; warning signs and escalation timing; source provenance when
+sources were consulted; and any blocker with its recovery owner. Keep health
+details in the response only as needed. Do not create files, modify clinical
+records, emit a diagnosis or prescription, or claim that external action was
+completed.
+
+## Completion Gate
+
+Complete only when the response addresses the stated goal, urgency was checked
+before routine advice, uncertainty is explicit, recommendations stay within
+the human boundaries, sensitive data is minimized, and current factual claims
+have authoritative provenance when required. Confirm that the invocation and
+identity remain `$health-workflow`, the bundle remains under the Health
+project surface, runtime visibility remains `project-local-inactive`, and no
+Foundry catalog entry or undeclared support artifact was created. Any required
+deterministic check, GPT-5.6 Sol evaluation, security check, or human review is
+owned by the outer harness and must pass before release.

+---
+name: coverage-workflow
+description: Use when a user needs to plan, assess, improve, or report software test coverage in the standalone coverage product, including selecting scope, interpreting coverage evidence, identifying meaningful gaps, and producing an approval-aware coverage action plan.
+---
+
+# Coverage Workflow
+
+## Overview
+
+Turn test-coverage evidence into a traceable assessment and a prioritized plan.
+Measure coverage only for the requested product and revision, distinguish
+uncovered behavior from merely uncovered lines, and recommend tests that reduce
+real risk. Do not treat a percentage target as proof of correctness, invent
+results, edit application code, change quality gates, publish reports, or merge
+changes unless the operator separately authorizes those actions.
+
+This is a standalone product skill. It is callable as `$coverage-workflow`, is
+owned by `coverage-product`, runs in Codex, has `standalone-only` visibility,
+and must not be added to the Foundry catalog.
+
+## Required Context
+
+Before assessing coverage, load the repository policies that govern the target
+files and testing commands, the operator's requested revision and scope, and
+the relevant test and source layout. When available, also load:
+
+- the coverage report together with the command, tool version, configuration,
+  thresholds, timestamp, and revision that produced it;
+- existing tests, test conventions, risk or requirements records, and prior
+  coverage baselines for the same scope;
+- changed-file or diff evidence when the request concerns patch coverage; and
+- CI failure output when the workflow was triggered by a coverage gate.
+
+Prefer machine-readable reports over screenshots or copied summaries. Keep
+generated evidence separate from prior reports, and identify every source by
+path, revision, or supplied artifact label. Never read secrets or unrelated
+product data merely because they are reachable.
+
+## Input Contract
+
+Require:
+
+- a target repository or supplied source-and-test artifact;
+- an explicit scope such as a package, component, changed lines, or whole
+  product; and
+- an objective: assess, compare, plan improvements, explain a gate failure, or
+  prepare a coverage report.
+
+Accept an existing coverage report or permission to run the repository's
+documented local coverage command. Optional inputs are a baseline revision or
+report, desired metric and threshold, risk priorities, excluded/generated
+paths, output format, and a test-writing request.
+
+Reject or pause on these invalid combinations:
+
+- a comparison request without two compatible revisions or reports;
+- a threshold claim without a named metric and scope;
+- reports produced with incompatible tools, configurations, filters, or source
+  maps unless the mismatch is explicitly normalized and disclosed;
+- a request to claim coverage from stale, partial, or unverifiable evidence;
+- requested writes outside the operator-approved repository and paths; or
+- a test-writing request whose required production behavior is ambiguous.
+
+If scope is omitted, propose the smallest scope supported by the request and
+ask the operator to confirm it before running expensive commands or writing
+tests.
+
+## Workflow
+
+1. Restate the objective, repository, revision, scope, metric, threshold or
+   baseline, permitted commands, and permitted write paths. Record unresolved
+   assumptions rather than silently expanding scope.
+2. Inspect repository policy, coverage configuration, source layout, and test
+   layout. Determine the authoritative command and whether it measures lines,
+   branches, functions, statements, mutations, or patch coverage.
+3. Validate supplied evidence: match its revision, scope, tool configuration,
+   timestamp, included and excluded paths, source maps, and command provenance.
+   Mark evidence stale or incomparable when any material property differs.
+4. If evidence is absent and local execution is authorized, run only the
+   documented coverage command. Preserve the exact command, exit status, and
+   report locations. Do not weaken thresholds or alter configuration to obtain
+   a passing result.
+5. Extract totals and per-file or per-component gaps. Map uncovered locations
+   back to behavior and classify each as critical path, error handling,
+   boundary case, integration seam, defensive or unreachable code, generated
+   code, or unknown.
+6. Inspect nearby implementation and existing tests before recommending work.
+   Identify duplicate-test risk, brittle implementation coupling, and cases
+   where branch, mutation, contract, or integration evidence is more useful
+   than additional line coverage.
+7. Prioritize gaps by product risk, likelihood, blast radius, observability,
+   and test cost. For every recommended test, name the behavior, setup, action,
+   observable assertion, intended coverage gap, and suitable test level.
+8. When test creation is explicitly authorized, make only scoped test or
+   fixture changes. Keep production-code changes separate and require distinct
+   authorization. Run the narrowest relevant tests before any broader suite.
+9. Reproduce coverage with the same configuration, compare like-for-like
+   results, and report regressions as well as improvements. Attribute a change
+   only when the evidence supports causation.
+10. Produce the output contract, list approvals still required, and hand off
+    without publishing, merging, changing gates, or asserting completion of
+    checks owned by CI or a human reviewer.
+
+## Decision Points
+
+| Situation | Route | Default-deny behavior |
+| --- | --- | --- |
+| Valid current report exists | Analyze the report and inspect relevant code/tests | Do not rerun costly suites without need |
+| No report; documented local command is authorized | Run it and preserve provenance | Do not guess commands or install tools |
+| Reports are incompatible | Report each separately or request a matched rerun | Do not calculate a misleading delta |
+| Overall percentage is high but critical behavior is untested | Prioritize behavior and risk | Do not declare adequate coverage from totals alone |
+| Uncovered code appears unreachable or generated | Request evidence for exclusion or removal | Do not silently exclude it |
+| Tests require production changes | Split the proposal and request approval | Do not modify production code |
+| CI, credentials, paid services, or external systems are required | Prepare a handoff for the authorized operator | Do not trigger external effects |
+
+For ambiguous scope, uncertain provenance, or conflicting repository policy,
+stop at an evidence inventory and ask for a decision. The narrowest approved
+scope is the default.
+
+## Failure Handling
+
+Use stable blockers in the report:
+
+- `coverage-scope-missing`: no assessable component, revision, or path set;
+- `coverage-evidence-missing`: neither a valid report nor an authorized command;
+- `coverage-evidence-stale`: report does not match the target revision;
+- `coverage-results-incomparable`: baseline and candidate configurations differ;
+- `coverage-command-failed`: the authoritative command did not produce valid evidence;
+- `coverage-policy-conflict`: the requested action violates repository policy;
+- `coverage-write-unapproved`: requested edits exceed approved paths or roles;
+- `coverage-external-approval-required`: CI, credentials, publishing, or another consequential action needs an operator; and
+- `coverage-verification-failed`: relevant tests or reproduced coverage failed.
+
+On failure, stop the affected branch, preserve original evidence, state what
+was observed without converting missing data into zero coverage, and name the
+recovery input and its owner. Never delete a failing report, suppress a gate,
+or rewrite baseline evidence to manufacture a pass.
+
+## Human Boundaries
+
+The operator retains approval for scope expansion, dependency installation,
+long-running or paid execution, access to private systems, production-code
+changes, coverage exclusions, threshold or gate changes, CI reruns, external
+writes, publication, commits, merges, and release decisions. Repository policy
+may impose stricter boundaries and always wins.
+
+This workflow may inspect in-scope files, analyze supplied reports, and run
+explicitly authorized local commands. It may write tests only when requested
+and only in approved paths. Recommendations are not permission to perform a
+consequential action.
+
+## Output Contract
+
+Return a coverage assessment containing:
+
+- objective, repository, revision, scope, metric, tool/configuration identity,
+  and evidence timestamp;
+- exact evidence sources and, when run, command, exit status, and report paths;
+- current totals and scoped details, with baseline deltas only when comparable;
+- a risk-ranked gap inventory linked to files, locations, and behaviors;
+- proposed or implemented tests with test level, observable assertions, and
+  expected gap addressed;
+- changed files and verification results when edits were authorized;
+- limitations, excluded paths with justification, blockers, and remaining
+  approvals; and
+- a handoff that clearly separates measured facts, inferences, and proposals.
+
+Do not emit credentials, raw private data, fabricated metrics, or a claim that
+percentage improvement proves product correctness.
+
+## Completion Gate
+
+Complete the requested workflow only when identities and scope are explicit;
+evidence matches the target revision and configuration; results are
+reproducible or clearly labeled as supplied-only; every numeric claim traces to
+a report; recommendations trace to observed gaps and product risk; authorized
+edits stay in bounds; and relevant tests plus coverage verification pass.
+
+Also require repository-mandated deterministic checks, a model-run evaluation
+when the owning harness requires one, human review for coverage-policy or
+release decisions, and security review for tests involving secrets, sensitive
+fixtures, authorization boundaries, or external systems. If any gate is owned
+by CI, the outer harness, or a reviewer and has not run, report `partial` or
+`blocked` rather than claiming it passed.
